title: Episode 6

jokes.text: Long silence followed by 'I just can't believe you did that' — the entire opening scene is Fleabag and the Priest post-coitus, with the camera/audience inference that something enormous has just happened between them, and the show withholds it as comedic tension
jokes.setup: The transcript opens with 'What?' and a long gap, with ellipses and hesitations stretched across nearly 30 seconds
jokes.punchline: 'I just can't believe you did that.' — the understated delivery after prolonged mutual stunned silence implies something transgressive and delicious happened, and the restraint IS the joke
jokes.explanation: The show's entire second season has built to this moment of consummation between Fleabag and the Priest, and Waller-Bridge delivers the comedy entirely through withholding. The joke is structural — the audience has been waiting for this and the show responds with near-silence and fumbling half-sentences. The understatement 'I just can't believe you did that' is perfectly calibrated: it could mean anything and means everything. High craft because the economy is immaculate — every pause earns its place.

jokes.text: Claire on Klare: 'Well, he's crazy about me so that's a nightmare.' / 'Nightmare.' (Fleabag echoing dryly)
jokes.setup: Someone asks how Klare is. Expected answer: fine, good, complicated.
jokes.punchline: Claire reports that the man she loves being 'crazy about her' is a nightmare. Fleabag echoes 'Nightmare' perfectly — not sarcastically but as genuine sisterly solidarity.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. Claire's emotional repression is so complete that being loved is categorized as a problem. And Fleabag's single-word echo 'Nightmare' isn't mockery — it's perfect sisterly fluency. Could only work with these two specific characters. The economy is flawless. Scores 8+ because the character integration is near-perfect and the joke is genuinely quotable.

jokes.text: 'It's gonna be a lovely day, isn't it?' / 'I'm afraid so.'
jokes.setup: Standard pleasantry about the wedding day being lovely
jokes.punchline: 'I'm afraid so' — a sincere dread disguised as polite agreement, revealing that both sisters are dreading the day
jokes.explanation: Five words perfectly encapsulating the sisters' shared dread of the Godmother and this wedding. The subversion of a stock pleasantry with existential resignation is precisely the kind of deadpan economy Fleabag does brilliantly. Claire's delivery of 'I'm afraid so' is quintessentially her character — emotionally controlled, darkly funny, and deeply sad. Near-perfect economy score because not a single syllable is wasted.

jokes.text: Godmother introduces her friends: 'This is my very interesting friend Daniel, who is deaf... And this is my extraordinary friend Francine. She's a lesbian. And this is Asif, my bisexual Syrian refugee friend...'
jokes.setup: The Godmother is introducing her eclectic group of friends at a wedding party
jokes.punchline: Each introduction reduces the person to a single identity marker — deaf, lesbian, bisexual Syrian refugee — with escalating absurdity, presented with complete sincerity as 'fascinating' credentials
jokes.explanation: This is a sharp character comedy joke that exposes the Godmother's performative liberalism — she doesn't have friends, she has trophies. The escalation from 'deaf' to 'lesbian' to 'bisexual Syrian refugee' builds perfectly, each step more absurdly reductive. It's deeply character-specific: only the Godmother could deliver this with such complete self-satisfaction. The cringe comes from recognizing the type immediately.

jokes.text: 'Oh, my God. This is... This is... God, how extraordinary. I just... I always call you "darling." This is the love of my life.'
jokes.setup: The Godmother is introducing her partner to her eclectic friend Asif
jokes.punchline: She has been introducing her friends to her partner but realizes she doesn't know her own partner's name — she always calls him 'darling' — and covers with the most overwrought declaration possible
jokes.explanation: The joke is that the Godmother doesn't know her own husband's name in the context of formal introduction, which she covers by escalating to 'the love of my life.' It's perfectly character-integrated — this woman reduces everyone else to identity labels but can't name her own husband — and the self-importance of the cover is classically her. The structural pause works well.

jokes.text: 'I'm going to open it over a bin so I've got somewhere to put the paper.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag brings a present to the wedding despite being told no presents; the Godmother says 'you're such a sweetheart' and 'she's a natural rule breaker'
jokes.punchline: Rather than the expected gracious acceptance or meaningful sentiment, the Godmother plans to open it over a bin to dispose of the wrapping — simultaneously passive-aggressive, practical, and dismissive
jokes.explanation: Deceptively good line. The Godmother's passive aggression is so complete she can't even fake warmth for a full sentence. The specificity of 'over a bin' makes it feel real and cutting. Character-specific: only the Godmother would say this at her own wedding, and only to Fleabag.

jokes.text: 'I just wondered if you had a little show planned... Well, you normally do.'
jokes.setup: The Godmother pulls Fleabag aside privately
jokes.punchline: Instead of a heartfelt moment, she's pre-emptively warning Fleabag not to ruin the wedding with one of her 'shows' — i.e., catastrophes — which is simultaneously insulting and, given Fleabag's track record, not unreasonable
jokes.explanation: Functional joke that works on multiple levels — the Godmother is insulting Fleabag while also being genuinely prudent. The term 'little show' is nicely condescending. Good character work but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'D'you know, I often thought it strange that of all my pieces you chose to take her. Why? — She was based on your mother. So nice to have her back in the house.'
jokes.setup: The Godmother gives back a piece of art — ostensibly gracious — and Fleabag's reaction implies she took the sculpture because it was meaningful
jokes.punchline: 'She was based on your mother. So nice to have her back in the house.' — The Godmother reveals this deliberately with perfect timing, turning the gift return into an act of psychological warfare
jokes.explanation: Devastating. The show's entire emotional architecture is built on Fleabag's grief for her dead mother, and the Godmother — who has married the father — uses a sculpture of the mother as a weapon of possession. 'So nice to have her back in the house' is chilling because it's true on multiple levels: she means the sculpture, but she has also replaced the mother in the house. The joke is in the smiling cruelty. Character integration is exceptional — this is a uniquely Godmother move.

jokes.text: 'I thought you were a fox.' / 'No.' / 'Are you...?'
jokes.setup: The Priest is startled in the garden while rehearsing his homily; Fleabag emerges suddenly
jokes.punchline: He thought she was a fox — a complete non sequitur that deflates the tension of their post-coital reunion with absurd mundanity
jokes.explanation: A small, well-executed beat. The emotional charge of this first face-to-face meeting after the night before is immediately undercut by him mistaking her for a fox. The absurdity works because the Priest is such an earnest, slightly otherworldly figure that this feels plausible, and the anti-climax is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: 'You look lovely.' / 'Thank you. So do you.' / 'Wait till you see me in the full... shebang. You're gonna lose your fucking mind.'
jokes.setup: They exchange sincere compliments in what is clearly a loaded moment of morning-after awkwardness
jokes.punchline: 'Wait till you see me in the full... shebang. You're gonna lose your fucking mind.' — The Priest, supposedly celibate and currently in street clothes, drops 'fucking' and uses this delighted, cocky phrase about his vestments, which is both endearingly vain and reveals the attractive self-awareness he tries to suppress
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's great small jokes. The Priest knows he's attractive in vestments and cannot resist saying so, which is simultaneously charming, vain, and completely incongruous given what has just happened between them. 'You're gonna lose your fucking mind' is perfectly calibrated — the casual 'fucking' from a priest, the giddy self-confidence, the vanity about religious robes. It's character-defining: he's a man of God who knows he looks good in the outfit. High character integration because this joke only works from him.

jokes.text: 'We just need to get through this bit, and then we can...' [pause] 'We can...' / 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: After the loaded 'you're gonna lose your mind' exchange, the Priest tries to articulate what comes after the wedding
jokes.punchline: Neither character can finish the sentence. The unspoken recognition that there IS no 'and then we can' — that they have no future — lands in the silence
jokes.explanation: This is the show doing what it does best: comedy from silence and incompletion. The half-sentence 'We can...' trails into nothing and 'Yeah' accepts the nothing. It's painfully funny and painful in equal measure. The craft is in the gap — four words do the work of a scene. Economy score is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Fleabag and another character notice lipstick smeared somewhere visible — 'You have lipstick all...' / 'Oh, fucking hell. That would not look good.'
jokes.setup: The morning-after encounter in the garden
jokes.punchline: Lipstick evidence of the night before is visible on one of them (likely Fleabag from the Priest), requiring urgent concealment minutes before a wedding — the physical evidence of transgression treated as a logistical problem
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe comedy — the visible evidence of the illicit night creates stakes and embarrassment. The 'that would not look good' is an understatement that works. Not exceptional but solid situational comedy.

jokes.text: 'I don't know what this feeling is. Is it God, or is it me?' / 'I don't know.'
jokes.setup: After a moment of emotional and physical connection, Fleabag articulates her confusion
jokes.punchline: She genuinely cannot distinguish between religious ecstasy and sexual/romantic feeling, and asks the Priest — whose entire crisis is exactly this — and he admits he doesn't know either
jokes.explanation: This is the thematic core of the entire season expressed as a joke. Fleabag asking whether her transcendent feeling is God or love, and the Priest — whose vocation IS to know — saying he doesn't know either, is simultaneously the funniest and most devastating line in the episode. It works as a joke because the absurdity is complete: she's asking the one person who should know the difference, and he's as lost as she is. Extraordinary character integration — this only works between these two characters in this situation. The season's entire argument about love, faith, and transcendence is compressed into one exchange. Among the best jokes in the series.

jokes.text: 'Fuck you, then.'
jokes.setup: The silence after 'I don't know' — the episode implies this is directed at God, or possibly herself, or the situation
jokes.punchline: 'Fuck you, then.' — said apparently to nothing and no one visible, which in context reads as directed at God or the universe, delivered with the Fleabag-to-camera beat
jokes.explanation: Three words. The entire season's tension between Fleabag and her feelings — whether for the Priest or God or life itself — resolved in one profane dismissal. The comedy is in the scale inversion: saying 'fuck you' to the universe/God/love with the same tone you'd use to dismiss a slow waiter. Perfect economy (10/10 — you literally cannot do it in fewer words), perfect character voice, devastatingly funny and sad. One of the most quotable lines in the series.

jokes.text: 'I hate my husband and the man I love is on his way to Finland, so pretty weird.'
jokes.setup: 'Are you OK?' — a standard social pleasantry at a wedding
jokes.punchline: Claire delivers a devastatingly compressed summary of her emotional catastrophe with 'so pretty weird' as the sign-off, treating marital collapse and lost love as a mildly unusual day
jokes.explanation: Claire at her peak. The compression of 'I hate my husband and the man I love is on his way to Finland' followed by 'so pretty weird' is a masterclass in the Fleabag tonal register: catastrophic emotional content delivered with English social minimization. Only Claire — buttoned-up, emotionally withholding, brutally competent — could make this as funny as it is devastating. 'Pretty weird' as the landing is perfect economy.

jokes.text: 'Oh, way to upstage the bride!' / 'Tried my best.'
jokes.setup: Claire has just confessed marital collapse and heartbreak at the wedding
jokes.punchline: Fleabag frames it as competitive upstaging, Claire accepts the framing with 'tried my best' — both sisters turning pain into deadpan banter instantly
jokes.explanation: The sisters' dynamic crystallized in two lines — they process agony through deadpan competition. 'Tried my best' is the correct Claire response. Good economy and strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'This is our very chic priest.' / 'Oh, good, I was aiming for chic.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is introduced in vestments — 'the full shebang' he promised
jokes.punchline: He confirms he was aiming for chic, which is a vain, slightly absurd admission for a priest about his vestments — callbacks his 'you're gonna lose your fucking mind' vanity
jokes.explanation: Pays off the earlier joke perfectly. His vestment vanity is a running character note and 'I was aiming for chic' confirms it — a priest whose aesthetic ambitions include looking fashionable at weddings. The callback elevates it.

jokes.text: 'And this is my unstable step-daughter who's had a miscarriage.'
jokes.setup: The Godmother is introducing Fleabag to the Priest in the same parade-of-identity-labels format she used earlier
jokes.punchline: Fleabag is introduced as 'my unstable step-daughter who's had a miscarriage' — applying the same reductive identity-label logic to Fleabag as she did to her diverse friend group, but making it personally cutting
jokes.explanation: The callback to the identity-label introduction format is devastating here — Fleabag gets the same treatment as 'interesting Daniel who is deaf' but her label is 'unstable' and 'had a miscarriage.' The Godmother's complete unselfconsciousness makes it worse and funnier. Strong character integration and the callback structure elevates it significantly.

jokes.text: Priest introduces himself to Martin: 'Bit nervous, Martin' — completely honest, completely wrong-footing
jokes.setup: Martin asks the Priest 'How you doing, man?' expecting a social pleasantry
jokes.punchline: The Priest answers completely honestly — 'Bit nervous, Martin' — treating a social formula as a real question
jokes.explanation: Small but effective — the Priest's radical honesty in social situations is a consistent character trait. Using 'Bit nervous' where 'Fine, thanks' is expected deflates small talk cleanly.

jokes.text: 'It was my miscarriage.' / [pause] / 'It was my fucking miscarriage.'
jokes.setup: The Godmother has introduced Fleabag as having had a miscarriage; the Priest says 'but you knew that, you were there'; Claire corrects the record
jokes.punchline: Claire's quiet first correction — 'It was my miscarriage' — lands in silence; the second delivery with 'fucking' added reveals the depth of feeling she has suppressed about this
jokes.explanation: A season-long deception unravels in one line repeated twice with escalating profanity. The comedy is in the escalation: the first delivery is almost politely corrective; the second with 'fucking' inserted is Claire's emotional dam breaking. It's a double-punch that works as comedy (the sudden profanity from buttoned-up Claire) and as drama (the weight of what she's been carrying). The structural choice to repeat it is immaculate. Among the episode's finest moments.

jokes.text: 'Yes, I thought you'd find that funny.'
jokes.setup: After Claire's bombshell 'it was my fucking miscarriage' revelation
jokes.punchline: Claire says this apparently looking at Fleabag — who presumably has an involuntary reaction — and the line is perfectly placed sarcasm that also reveals Claire knows her sister finds dark things funny
jokes.explanation: A sharp beat that captures the sisters' dynamic perfectly — Claire knows Fleabag will react inappropriately to dark news and pre-empts it with sarcasm. It also slightly implicates the audience, who are also finding it funny.

jokes.text: 'I guess it was your baby's way of saying it didn't want you as its father. Like a goldfish out of the bowl sort of thing.'
jokes.setup: Martin is reeling from the revelation that Claire had his baby and miscarried without telling him
jokes.punchline: Claire compares the miscarriage to a goldfish choosing to leave its bowl — a blissfully inappropriate metaphor delivered with total sincerity, implying the embryo made a sentient decision to reject Martin as a father
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark comedy. The image of a foetus as a goldfish deliberately jumping out of its bowl to avoid being Martin's child is both hilarious and cutting. The specificity of 'like a goldfish out of the bowl sort of thing' is the key — the homely, domestic analogy applied to a miscarriage is Claire's emotional armor at its sharpest. High originality (the metaphor is genuinely surprising), strong structure, perfect character voice. One of the episode's standout lines.

jokes.text: 'Sorry, but whoever had a miscarriage could you take it to the kitchen, please?'
jokes.setup: An emotional confrontation about the miscarriage is unfolding at the wedding
jokes.punchline: The Godmother treats the revelation of a miscarriage as a social management problem — 'whoever had a miscarriage' as if she's asking someone to move a bag — prioritizing the event over the catastrophe
jokes.explanation: The Godmother at her most perfectly monstrous. 'Whoever had a miscarriage' treats a devastating personal revelation as a catering inconvenience. The vagueness ('whoever') implies she barely tracks which of her step-daughters had it. The instruction to 'take it to the kitchen' as if it's a noisy conversation rather than existential family drama is both extremely funny and extremely revealing. High character integration — this is her character's most essential quality crystallized into one sentence.

jokes.text: Martin's speech about his virtues: 'I pick up Jake from shit. I make dessert for Easter. I organise the downstairs toilet. I fired the humming cleaner. I hoover the car. I put up all your certificates and I don't make you feel guilty for not having sex with me.'
jokes.setup: Martin is trying to persuade Claire not to leave him, listing his positive qualities
jokes.punchline: The list escalates through mundane domestic chores until it bottoms out at 'I don't make you feel guilty for not having sex with me' — the final item reveals the real state of their marriage
jokes.explanation: The comedic architecture of this list is expert. It starts with 'I pick up Jake from shit' (already undercutting the heroism), descends through increasingly trivial chores, then blindsides with 'I don't make you feel guilty for not having sex with me.' The pivot from domestic practicalities to sexual dysfunction reveals everything about their marriage. Martin is so earnest in his itemization that it's both desperately funny and genuinely sad. The escalation structure is well-calibrated.

jokes.text: 'You are an alcoholic, and you tried it on with my sister.' / 'Fine. I tried to kiss your sister on her birthday.' / 'My birthday!'
jokes.setup: Claire is listing Martin's faults
jokes.punchline: 'My birthday!' — the outrage is not just that he kissed her sister but that he did it on Claire's own birthday, which somehow makes it worse and more specifically awful
jokes.explanation: The structure is excellent — Martin's correction ('her birthday') is immediately overridden by Claire's correction ('my birthday'), which is both funnier and more damning. The specificity of the birthday escalates the transgression perfectly. Good callback structure for series veterans.

jokes.text: 'I am not a bad guy! I just have a bad personality. It's not my fault. Some people are born with fucked personalities.'
jokes.setup: Martin is making his case that he's not a bad person despite listing his faults
jokes.punchline: The distinction between 'bad guy' and 'bad personality' is philosophically absurd and self-serving; 'it's not my fault, some people are born with fucked personalities' as exculpation is both hilarious and logically circular
jokes.explanation: The philosophical gymnastics here are Martin's character perfectly expressed. The 'bad guy / bad personality' distinction is exactly the kind of self-serving sophistry an awful man would deploy, and 'some people are born with fucked personalities' as defense of himself is wonderful in its circular logic. High quotability and strong character integration.

jokes.text: Martin lists his domestic contributions: 'I pick up Jake from shit. I make dessert for Easter. I organise the downstairs toilet. I fired the humming cleaner.'
jokes.setup: Martin is defending himself as a good partner by listing everything he does.
jokes.punchline: The list is magnificently mundane — picking up Jake from 'shit' (vague but evocative), making Easter dessert, organizing a toilet — and then the gem: 'I fired the humming cleaner.' The specificity of 'humming cleaner' implies this was a major household event.
jokes.explanation: The list of marital contributions is a comedy staple, but 'I fired the humming cleaner' is genuinely brilliant. The cleaner's habit of humming has clearly been a household grievance, and Martin firing her is positioned as a major act of devotion. 'You enjoyed that' from Claire — confirming it was indeed a service — makes it perfect. Highly quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: 'Look at Jake. He is so creepy. It's not his fault.'
jokes.setup: Martin is defending the 'born with a fucked personality' thesis
jokes.punchline: He uses his own step-son Jake as evidence for his theory — 'he is so creepy, it's not his fault' — which is both a genuine insult to Jake and an inadvertent self-indictment
jokes.explanation: Martin's choice of Jake as his evidence is perfect — it reveals that he finds his own step-child creepy, that he has no social filter even in his most impassioned speech, and that his theory conveniently excuses both of them. The deadpan 'it's not his fault' mirrors exactly how he's excusing himself. Tight character work.

jokes.text: 'Why the bassoon?' / 'You want to know what the bassoon is? It's a cry for help!'
jokes.setup: Martin has gone on an emotional speech; suddenly pivots to the question of why Jake plays the bassoon
jokes.punchline: 'It's a cry for help!' — the bassoon, a famously unglamorous and awkward instrument, is now the symbol of Jake's alienation, and Martin delivering this insight mid-marital breakdown is absurdly funny
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's great lines. The specificity of 'the bassoon' is what makes this — not violin, not guitar, the bassoon. The sudden swerve from Martin's impassioned domestic grievance list to 'why the bassoon?' feels like a genuine non sequitur, but 'it's a cry for help!' delivers it as insight, which makes it funnier. The bassoon as cry for help is the most Martin interpretation possible. Very high quotability — this line has a life beyond the episode.

jokes.text: 'The main fucking problem here is that you don't like me.'
jokes.setup: After the entire speech — the virtues, the Jake theory, the bassoon — Martin distills the problem
jokes.punchline: After all that bluster, the real issue is not alcoholism or kissing her sister or the miscarriage but 'you don't like me' — the fundamental, undisguised wound
jokes.explanation: The pivot from the absurdist rampage to this stripped-down confession is precisely timed. The comedy comes from the shift in register and the bleak accuracy of the diagnosis. It's also funny because he says 'the main fucking problem' as if he's been doing triage and this is the result.

jokes.text: 'I love you. I make you laugh. I'm a douche, but I make you laugh. You said that that was the most important thing!'
jokes.setup: Martin is building toward his emotional conclusion
jokes.punchline: 'I'm a douche, but I make you laugh' — Martin's self-description as a 'douche' mid-declaration of love is his rock bottom self-awareness; the appeal to 'you said that was the most important thing' is both touching and pathetic
jokes.explanation: Martin's admission of 'I'm a douche' is his most self-aware moment and it's deployed in the most self-serving context possible. The comedy is in his using this partial self-awareness as a bargaining chip. 'You said that was the most important thing' carries the pain of someone who has held onto that line for years.

jokes.text: Claire gets on her knees and says 'Please leave me!' after Martin said he wouldn't leave until she was 'down on her knees and begging'
jokes.setup: Martin declares he will not leave until Claire is 'down on her knees and begging me' — implying begging him to stay
jokes.punchline: Claire immediately goes to her knees and begs him to leave — taking his dramatic condition completely literally and inverting its intended meaning
jokes.explanation: Structurally impeccable. Martin sets up a romantic movie cliché — 'I won't leave until you're on your knees begging me' — expecting her to eventually beg him to stay. Claire takes him at his word immediately, gets down on her knees, and begs him to leave. The inversion is perfect and the execution is pure Claire: she will use any available mechanism to get what she wants. The structure score is 9.0 because the setup-inversion is textbook perfection. Impact score near the top of the episode — this gets the biggest audience laugh.

jokes.text: 'I didn't think you'd do that in that dress.'
jokes.setup: Claire has just gotten on her knees in her formal wedding outfit to beg Martin to leave
jokes.punchline: Martin's response focuses on the dress rather than the emotional content — a perfectly deflated reaction from someone who has just been humiliated
jokes.explanation: Perfect capper to the kneeling joke. Martin's stunned deflation — he expected romantic pleading, got the opposite — is captured by his fixating on the practicality of the dress. It also slightly humanizes him: he genuinely hadn't predicted this. Good economy, well-placed.

jokes.text: Dad, trapped somewhere, suggests: 'Just give them all a bottle to take home. Honestly, they will be relieved.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has found Dad apparently stuck and unable to attend his own wedding; he says everyone will understand
jokes.punchline: His solution — a bottle each and send them home — treats his own wedding as an event people are relieved to escape from, with the confidence of someone who has considered this
jokes.explanation: Dad's resigned practicality about his own wedding is both funny and revealing. 'They will be relieved' is the key — he assumes everyone shares his ambivalence. It's warm and self-deprecating and very Dad. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Fleabag's reaction beat — 'Everyone will understand?' — she clearly cannot believe her father said that.
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been managing the wedding crisis, trying to help Dad get through it. She'd just been trying to help.
jokes.punchline: 'Everyone will understand?' — Fleabag echoing back Dad's acceptance of his own absence from his wedding with disbelief. The joke is that she's more invested in his wedding than he is.
jokes.explanation: A good reaction beat but less comic than the preceding line. The disbelief is real and funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Dad's elaborate cover story: 'I just remembered that I had left a friendly mousetrap up here a few weeks ago, and I hadn't checked it and I just wanted to make sure that one little chap hadn't got trapped in it and was suffocating up here.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks Dad what he's doing hiding upstairs at his own wedding
jokes.punchline: The mousetrap story is so specific, so elaborate, and so transparently false that it becomes a masterpiece of euphemism — 'a friendly mousetrap' is a particularly wonderful detail
jokes.explanation: The specificity of the lie is the joke — 'friendly mousetrap,' 'one little chap,' 'suffocating up here' — the more elaborate the cover story the more obvious the panic. 'Friendly mousetrap' is a delightful turn of phrase that reveals a man who cannot bear to harm anything, including mice, which is perfect character work for someone avoiding his wedding.

jokes.text: 'Do you wanna make a run for it? I can smuggle you out in one of Mum's dresses.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is offering Dad an escape from his own wedding
jokes.punchline: The offer to use 'Mum's dresses' as disguise is both a practical joke and a pointed one — the mother's dresses are presumably in the Godmother's house now, suggesting complicated territory
jokes.explanation: The mention of Mum's dresses is characteristically Fleabag — turning a practical escape plan into something emotionally loaded without drawing attention to it. The comedy is in the breezy offering of the absurd escape plan.

jokes.text: 'I love you, but I'm not sure that I like you all the time.' / 'Hey, you created this monster.' / 'You're not the way you are because of me.'
jokes.setup: Dad is telling Fleabag about her qualities
jokes.punchline: Dad's 'I love you but I'm not sure I like you all the time' is both a callback to Martin's earlier speech and a funny-bleak parental admission; Fleabag's 'you created this monster' is her immediate reach for self-deprecating comedy; Dad's correction is even funnier
jokes.explanation: The father-daughter dynamic here is quietly funny — he's honest about not always liking her, she deflects with 'you created this monster', and his correction 'you're not the way you are because of me' redirects to the mother. It's warm and sharp simultaneously, which is the Dad character's consistent register.

jokes.text: 'We are proud of you, Dad. You have two daughters who love you, even if you don't like them.' / 'I like Claire.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag reassures Dad of his daughters' love
jokes.punchline: 'I like Claire.' — Dad's immediate exception, delivered apparently without guile, undercuts the sentiment perfectly and is also, given what we know, exactly right
jokes.explanation: The setup-punchline here is structurally gorgeous. Fleabag says 'even if you don't like them' as a kind of generous self-deprecation, expecting Dad to protest — and he simply says 'I like Claire.' The deadpan exception is perfect: the pause before it, the guilelessness of it, and the accuracy of it (he does like Claire more) makes it land beautifully. 'Jesus, Dad!' is the ideal button. Very high economy — two words do enormous work.

jokes.text: 'Before we start, uh, Jake has asked to play another piece on his bassoon.'
jokes.setup: The wedding ceremony is beginning; guests are assembled
jokes.punchline: Jake's bassoon reappears as a running gag — the instrument's awfulness and its associations with 'cry for help' from Martin's earlier speech make this callback land
jokes.explanation: The running bassoon gag pays off Martin's 'cry for help' analysis. The Priest's 'uh, Jake has asked to play another piece' — the slight resignation in 'another' — is good deadpan delivery. The callback elevates it.

jokes.text: Claire's airport speech: 'You can't get through security without a boarding pass. No, I wasn't suggesting that — I'd have to buy a dummy ticket just to get through the gate. I don't know when his flight is or which terminal. Imagine if I knew that. Imagine him finding out I knew all that.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks if Klare warrants the 'running through the airport' gesture; Claire says she's not going
jokes.punchline: Claire's increasingly specific operational knowledge of what going to the airport would require — including 'a dummy ticket' to pass security and knowing which terminal — reveals she has absolutely thought this through in detail while insisting she hasn't
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is in the gap between Claire's stated position (I'm not going) and the operational specificity of her objections. 'I'd have to buy a dummy ticket just to get through the gate' — she knows airport security procedures well enough to have mapped out the plan she's definitely not executing. 'Imagine if I knew that' is the brilliant false protest of someone who absolutely knows. Pure character comedy: Claire's armor cracking while she insists it isn't.

jokes.text: 'Imagine if he was just in Boots buying a pair of tweezers in Terminal Five, and suddenly I was there. "Hello, Klare."'
jokes.setup: Continuation of Claire's airport fantasy she's definitely not having
jokes.punchline: The specific image — Terminal 5 Boots, buying tweezers — is exquisitely mundane for a grand romantic gesture; 'Hello, Klare' as the imagined line is perfectly flat for a rom-com climax
jokes.explanation: The Boots tweezers detail is genius. Claire's romantic fantasy is so specific and so mundane — not a dramatic gate-sprint but finding him in Boots buying tweezers — that it's both very funny and very Claire. And 'Hello, Klare' as her imagined grand declaration is perfectly deflated: this woman, who cannot express emotion, imagines interrupting someone's toiletry purchase with a flat greeting. High originality for the specific image chosen and high character integration.

jokes.text: 'The only person I'd run through an airport for is you.'
jokes.setup: The airport-running conversation between Fleabag and Claire, about Claire and Klare
jokes.punchline: Jake, who has been silently present, delivers this to — presumably — one of the sisters, which is both a complete non sequitur and a genuinely sweet moment from the character everyone calls creepy
jokes.explanation: Jake's sudden interjection is perfectly deployed — he's been the butt of jokes (creepy, bassoon as cry for help) and now delivers the episode's most straightforwardly romantic line, with no apparent awareness of the conversation. The comedy is in the whiplash: the character defined by everyone else as irredeemably weird turns out to be capable of guileless, sincere affection. Good misdirection structure and very high character integration.

jokes.text: 'Thank you, Jake, for that beautiful bassoon piece uh, written specially for today. I believe it's called... "Where's Claire?"'
jokes.setup: Jake has just played his bassoon at the wedding as the ceremony begins
jokes.punchline: The piece is titled 'Where's Claire?' — Jake's bassoon cry-for-help has been channeled into a composition about Claire's absence, which is both very funny and oddly moving
jokes.explanation: Magnificent callback convergence. The bassoon is a cry for help; Jake is creepy but sincere; Claire is missing; and the piece he composed is called 'Where's Claire?' It's funny, it's earned, and it retroactively makes every bassoon joke better. The structure is nearly perfect — it takes three separate running threads (the bassoon, Jake's strangeness, Claire's absence) and lands them in a single title. One of the episode's best constructed jokes.

jokes.text: The Priest begins his homily: 'Love is awful. It's awful. It's painful. It's frightening... It makes you creepy! It makes you obsessed with your hair!'
jokes.setup: The Priest begins a traditional wedding homily about love
jokes.punchline: The homily turns into a catalog of love's worst qualities — standard so far — but 'it makes you creepy' and 'it makes you obsessed with your hair' are clearly about specific people in the room (Jake; the Priest himself), delivered in a religious register
jokes.explanation: The homily works on multiple levels: as a genuine meditation on love's difficulty, as a list of recognizable afflictions affecting characters in the room, and as the Priest's barely concealed autobiographical confession. 'It makes you creepy' is a callback to Jake; 'obsessed with your hair' is the Priest on himself. The wedding audience hears a homily; the viewer gets the subtext.

jokes.text: Priest: 'So, it turns out it's quite hard to come up with something original to say about love but I've had a go.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is about to deliver the homily at a wedding
jokes.punchline: 'I've had a go' — lowering expectations for a sermon with extreme British understatement before delivering something extraordinary
jokes.explanation: 'I've had a go' is quintessentially British self-deprecation before a masterwork. The gap between the disclaimer and what follows is the joke — and knowing what follows makes this funnier in retrospect. Good character integration — the Priest's combination of vulnerability and quiet excellence.

jokes.text: 'There's something wrong with your priest.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is delivering an increasingly alarming homily about love being awful and frightening
jokes.punchline: Someone in the congregation observes flatly 'there's something wrong with your priest' — a perfect reaction beat that acknowledges what everyone is thinking
jokes.explanation: The straight-man reaction to the Priest's unraveling homily. 'There's something wrong with your priest' as a murmured aside is perfectly placed and perfectly phrased — 'your priest' as if assigning culpability to whoever brought him. Good economy, good timing.

jokes.text: 'Go out the side way. Now.'
jokes.setup: After delivering a homily that has revealed his feelings, the Priest apparently sees Fleabag and directs her to leave via the side exit
jokes.punchline: After the most emotionally raw speech of the episode, the Priest's practical direction — 'go out the side way, now' — is completely anti-climactic and perfectly in character: he's decisive about logistics even when emotionally destroyed
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the abruptness — after the most beautiful speech in the episode, 'go out the side way, now' is completely dry and practical. The Priest cannot afford to look at her any longer and his solution is logistical. Very high economy score: five words after a two-minute meditation on love.

jokes.text: 'Oh, fuck it.' (Godmother kisses Fleabag at the wedding)
jokes.setup: Post-ceremony, the Godmother and Fleabag have their final exchange. We expect either more manipulation or cold dismissal.
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, fuck it.' — and she kisses her. The Godmother, who has spent the episode being surgically cruel to Fleabag, has a moment of genuine warmth (or at least contact). The casualness of 'Oh, fuck it' before a kiss is the joke.
jokes.explanation: 'Oh, fuck it' as the preamble to an act of warmth from the most controlled character in the show is a genuinely funny subversion. The economy is perfect — three words that reframe their entire relationship.

jokes.text: 'Oh, the, uh, priest is looking for you.' / 'Oh.' / 'Don't break his heart.'
jokes.setup: Dad has noticed the dynamic between Fleabag and the Priest
jokes.punchline: 'Don't break his heart.' — Dad knows. He says nothing about the transgression, makes no speech, just delivers this gentle warning with full awareness
jokes.explanation: Dad knowing and saying it in four words is very good. The comedy is in the understatement and the implication: he's seen everything, he's not judging, he just doesn't want anyone else hurt. 'Don't break his heart' treats a priest and his daughter's impossible affair as simply a matter of human kindness. Beautiful economy.

jokes.text: 'Oh, are you leaving?' / 'I was changing.' / 'What do you do? Do you get the... get the bus, or...?' / 'Yeah, I get the bus. On the road? — Just on the road. I get on the bus.'
jokes.setup: The final farewell between Fleabag and the Priest after he has chosen God
jokes.punchline: Their final conversation — after a season of extraordinary emotional and intellectual intimacy — is entirely about whether he gets a bus. The mundane small talk as the form their goodbye takes is both very funny and devastating
jokes.explanation: This is exquisite. The conversation between two people who have been discussing God, love, transcendence, and sacrifice all season ends with an anxious mutual inquiry about bus routes. 'Just on the road. I get on the bus.' delivered with the awkward specificity of someone filling impossible silence. The comedy is entirely in the gap between what they feel and what they can say. Very high character integration — this is only possible at this moment between these two people.

jokes.text: 'Bye, Father.' / [pause] / '"Bye, Father."' [said again quietly to herself]
jokes.setup: Fleabag says goodbye to the Priest using his religious title
jokes.punchline: She repeats 'Bye, Father' to herself quietly, seeming to register the double meaning — he is literally 'Father' as a priest, and there's a dark comedy in using the formal religious title as your farewell to someone you love
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'Bye, Father' to herself is quietly devastating and quietly funny — the joke is that she's using the formal honorific for a man she loves, which is both appropriate (he's a priest) and a kind of joke at her own expense. The self-aware repetition, as if trying to process the absurdity, is Fleabag's characteristic mode.

jokes.text: 'You nailed it.'
jokes.setup: After the Priest has left; Fleabag apparently looks at the camera or says this to someone about the Priest's homily/day
jokes.punchline: 'You nailed it.' — the phrase, whether directed at herself, the Priest, or the camera, carries the full weight of irony: he did in fact nail it (the homily was extraordinary) but also absolutely did not nail anything (he's losing the woman he loves and she's losing him)
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum ironic load. The ambiguity of who she's talking to and what she means is part of the joke. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: 'It's God, isn't it?' / 'Yeah.' / 'Damn.'
jokes.setup: Someone observes that the Priest and Fleabag's dynamic is clearly about something larger — whether his draw is God or her
jokes.punchline: Fleabag confirms it's God. 'Damn.' — the single word encapsulating the entirety of her loss, with the brevity of someone who has made peace with it and the comedy of English understatement applied to cosmic defeat
jokes.explanation: The episode's most distilled joke. The whole season is about Fleabag losing the Priest to God — and the entire tragedy is resolved in 'Damn.' Maximum economy: one word answers the question of who she lost him to and what she feels about it. The comedy is in the scale inversion: you've lost someone to an omnipotent force, and your response is 'Damn' — the same word you'd use if you dropped your keys. Yet it's entirely genuine and entirely enough. Near-perfect scores justified.

jokes.text: 'You know, the worst thing is... that I fucking love you.'
jokes.setup: Their final conversation at the bus stop
jokes.punchline: 'The worst thing is that I fucking love you' — framing love as the worst thing that has happened to her is both the season's thesis and a genuinely funny inversion of romantic convention
jokes.explanation: Framing 'I love you' as 'the worst thing' is structurally perfect for Fleabag — love has always been pain for her, and this is the most honest possible declaration. The 'fucking' makes it real rather than romantic-movie. This is one of the most quoted lines from the series and deserves it: it's funny and heartbreaking in equal measure.

jokes.text: 'No, no, don't. No, let's just leave that out there, just for a second on its own.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has said 'I love you'; the Priest's instinct is presumably to respond or redirect
jokes.punchline: He asks her not to qualify or continue — 'let's just leave that out there, just for a second on its own' — treating the declaration as something to be preserved in amber rather than acted upon
jokes.explanation: This is the Priest at his most distinctly himself — the man who thinks carefully about language, meaning, and what should be preserved. Asking to leave 'I love you' out there for a second before responding is both philosophically interesting and gently funny: it's a request to pause the scene, to not move forward. High character integration because only this particular priest, with this particular relationship to language and silence, would say this.

jokes.text: 'It'll pass.'
jokes.setup: After sitting with 'I love you' for a moment, the Priest responds
jokes.punchline: 'It'll pass.' — the most brutally pragmatic response to 'I love you' imaginable, which is simultaneously self-protective, honest, and devastating
jokes.explanation: Two words. The response to 'I love you' is 'it'll pass' — which is a joke (in the sense that it's the least romantic possible response) and a tragedy (he means it, hopes it, fears it, uses it as armor). The economy is perfect — maximum craft, maximum impact, minimum words. This is the Fleabag mode at its finest: a line that makes you laugh and then immediately undoes the laugh. Among the series' most quoted lines.

jokes.text: 'This bus is not magically coming.' / 'I think I'll walk.'
jokes.setup: The emotional finale has just played out; they are still at the bus stop
jokes.punchline: The return to the bus — 'this bus is not magically coming' after earlier insisting it would — is a gentle joke that grounds everything back in ordinary life, and 'I think I'll walk' is the pragmatic decision following the most emotionally wrenching scene of the season
jokes.explanation: The callback to the bus-that-won't-come has a metaphorical resonance — she was also waiting for something that won't come. Phoebe Waller-Bridge's choice to return to bus logistics after the most devastating scene of the episode is characteristic of the show's tonal intelligence. Not exceptional comedy on its own but earns its place.

jokes.text: 'Uh, see you Sunday? / I'm joking, you're never ever allowed in my church again.'
jokes.setup: Their final farewell; he suggests seeing her Sunday
jokes.punchline: He immediately retracts with 'I'm joking, you're never ever allowed in my church again' — which is both self-protective and funny in its specificity: not 'we shouldn't see each other' but 'you are banned from my church'
jokes.explanation: The joke is structured perfectly: the 'see you Sunday' teases a future, the immediate correction closes it. But 'you're never ever allowed in my church again' as the prohibition is specifically funny — it's the professional boundary (this is my church, you can't come back) rather than the personal one (I can't see you). It treats their love affair as something that has violated sacred space, which is simultaneously funny and exactly right. High character integration — only this priest would phrase the ending this way.

jokes.text: 'I love you, too.'
jokes.setup: After banning her from his church, the Priest says this as she begins to leave
jokes.punchline: It lands as a punchline rather than a declaration — following 'you're never ever allowed in my church again' with 'I love you, too' is the show's tonal signature: the most important thing said last, quietly, after the logistics
jokes.explanation: The structure is near-perfect. Everything has been said, the goodbye managed, the ban issued — and then, as she walks away: 'I love you, too.' Placed after 'you're never allowed in my church' it lands as both punchline and gut-punch. The comedy is in the ordering: the most profound thing comes after the most practical prohibition. The show's entire tonal register in three words. Economy score is 10.

jokes.text: 'He went that way.' — Fleabag pointing to indicate the Priest's direction to the audience/camera in the final fourth-wall break
jokes.setup: The closing sequence; Fleabag has just said goodbye to the Priest and is walking away; the camera (audience) follows her
jokes.punchline: 'He went that way.' — Fleabag's final fourth-wall break, pointing the camera/audience away from her and toward the Priest, releasing them from following her story. It's both a joke and the show's final goodbye to its audience
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and arguably one of the finest final jokes in television comedy. The fourth-wall breaking device has been Fleabag's coping mechanism throughout both series — she uses the audience as her confidant, her escape. In this final moment, she does the opposite: she directs them away from her. 'He went that way' is simultaneously a joke (pointing the camera elsewhere like misdirecting a friend at a party) and the closing of the contract between Fleabag and her audience. She no longer needs them; she can survive this without narrating it. The comedy and the poignancy are inseparable. Perfect economy — four words that end a series. Character integration is 10: no other character in television could deliver this specific joke. This scores 9.8 craft because it synthesizes the entire series' formal device into a final punchline.

tmdb_overview: As the day of the wedding arrives, Jake wants to know where Claire is, and the priest upstages the bride. As secrets are revealed and decisions are made, will Fleabag find the ending she’s looking for?
